expanded_description:
Economic growth—the increase in output per person over time—is determined by a combination of factors. The Solow growth model identifies three primary drivers: saving (and investment), population growth, and technological progress. Saving and investment increase the capital stock, raising output per worker. Population growth dilutes the capital stock (more workers sharing the same capital), reducing output per worker. Technological progress (innovation, productivity improvements) shifts the production function upward, allowing sustained increases in output per worker even with constant capital per worker. In addition to these, other factors such as human capital (education, skills), institutions (property rights, rule of law), trade openness, and natural resources also affect growth.

Understanding the factors affecting growth is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others, for evaluating policies to promote growth, and for understanding the long-run determinants of living standards. The Solow model shows that saving and population growth affect the level of output per worker but not the long-run growth rate (which is determined by technological progress). This implies that sustained growth requires continuous innovation and productivity improvement.

In this section, we will analyze the effects of saving and investment on capital accumulation and growth, examine how population growth affects output per worker (dilution), discuss the role of technological progress as the engine of sustained growth, explore the concept of human capital and its contribution to growth, and evaluate the importance of institutions and trade. You will learn to calculate the effects of changes in saving rates and population growth on steady-state output, understand why technology is the ultimate source of long-run growth, and evaluate policies to boost growth.

core_concept.principle:
Economic growth is determined by three primary factors in the Solow model: saving (investment), population growth, and technological progress. Other factors (human capital, institutions, trade) also play important roles.

1. Saving and Investment (Capital Accumulation):

• Higher saving rate ($s$ ↑) → higher steady-state capital per worker ($k^*$) and output per worker ($y^*$).
• Effect is a level effect, not a growth effect (growth increases temporarily during transition).
• Diminishing returns to capital mean that the impact of saving decreases as $k$ rises.
• The golden rule saving rate maximizes steady-state consumption ($s_{gold} = \alpha$ for Cobb-Douglas).

2. Population Growth:

• Higher population growth ($n$ ↑) → lower steady-state $k^*$ and $y^*$ (dilution effect).
• Capital must be spread across more workers, reducing capital per worker.
• In the Solow model with population growth, $\Delta k = s f(k) - (\delta + n)k$.
• Countries with high population growth tend to be poorer (all else equal).

3. Technological Progress:

• Technological progress ($g$ ↑) → sustained growth in output per worker.
• Shifts the production function upward (more output from same inputs).
• In the Solow model with technology, steady-state growth rate of output per worker equals $g$.
• Technology is the ultimate source of long-run growth.

4. Human Capital (Education, Skills):

• Augmented Solow model includes human capital ($H$).
• $Y = K^\alpha H^\beta (AL)^{1-\alpha-\beta}$.
• Higher human capital increases output and steady-state income.
• Explains why countries with better education grow faster.

5. Institutions:

• Property rights, rule of law, contract enforcement, honest government.
• Good institutions encourage investment and innovation.
• Poor institutions (corruption, expropriation) deter growth.

6. Trade Openness:

• Access to global markets increases competition, technology transfer, and scale.
• Open economies tend to grow faster than closed economies.

7. Natural Resources:

• Resource-rich countries can grow faster (e.g., Canada, Australia) but may suffer from Dutch disease.
• Resource curse: some resource-rich countries have poor institutions and slow growth.

Policy Implications:

• Promote saving and investment (tax incentives, infrastructure).
• Control population growth (family planning, education).
• Invest in R&D and technology adoption (innovation policy, education).
• Build human capital (education, training).
• Strengthen institutions (rule of law, property rights, anti-corruption).
• Open to trade (free trade agreements, reduce barriers).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine two countries that are identical except for their saving rates. Country A saves 10% of its income; Country B saves 30%. Country B will have a higher steady-state capital stock and higher output per worker. But the growth rate during the transition will be higher for Country B as it accumulates capital. However, once both reach their steady states, growth stops (without technology). This is the level effect of saving.

Now consider population growth. Country C has 1% population growth; Country D has 3% population growth. Country D's capital stock must be spread across more workers, so its steady-state capital per worker and output per worker are lower. This is the dilution effect. Countries with high population growth (e.g., many African countries) tend to be poorer.

Technological progress is different. When technology improves, the production function shifts up. Output per worker can increase even with constant capital per worker. This allows sustained growth. In the Solow model with technological progress, the steady-state growth rate of output per worker equals the rate of technological progress. This is why technology is the ultimate source of long-run growth.

Human capital (education, skills) works similarly to physical capital. Countries with better-educated workers produce more output. The augmented Solow model includes human capital, explaining why countries like South Korea invested heavily in education and grew rapidly.

Institutions matter because they affect the incentives to invest and innovate. Countries with secure property rights and rule of law attract investment; countries with corruption and expropriation deter it. This is why North Korea is poor and South Korea is rich, despite similar starting points.

For Canada, growth is driven by saving (about 20-25% of GDP), immigration (population growth), technological progress (R&D, innovation), human capital (education), good institutions, and trade openness (USMCA). Canada's productivity gap with the US suggests that Canada lags in technological progress and innovation.

image.alt: Diagram: Factors affecting growth (saving, population, technology, human capital, institutions, trade, natural resources) leading to economic growth.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.1: Multiple factors affect economic growth: saving and investment, population growth, technological progress, human capital, institutions, trade openness, and natural resources.

sections.introduction:
Economic growth—the increase in output per person over time—is determined by a combination of factors. The Solow growth model identifies three primary drivers: saving (and investment), population growth, and technological progress. Saving and investment increase the capital stock, raising output per worker. Population growth dilutes the capital stock (more workers sharing the same capital), reducing output per worker. Technological progress (innovation, productivity improvements) shifts the production function upward, allowing sustained increases in output per worker even with constant capital per worker. In addition to these, other factors such as human capital (education, skills), institutions (property rights, rule of law), trade openness, and natural resources also affect growth.

Understanding the factors affecting growth is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others, for evaluating policies to promote growth, and for understanding the long-run determinants of living standards. The Solow model shows that saving and population growth affect the level of output per worker but not the long-run growth rate (which is determined by technological progress). This implies that sustained growth requires continuous innovation and productivity improvement.

In this section, we will analyze the effects of saving and investment on capital accumulation and growth, examine how population growth affects output per worker (dilution), discuss the role of technological progress as the engine of sustained growth, explore the concept of human capital and its contribution to growth, and evaluate the importance of institutions and trade.

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Contributions to GDP growth in Canada (capital, labor, TFP) over time; TFP (technology) is the most important long-run driver.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 13.4.2: Decomposition of GDP growth in Canada: capital accumulation, labor growth, and TFP (technology). TFP is the most important driver of long-run growth.

key_concept.definition:
Factors Affecting Economic Growth:

1. Saving and Investment (Capital Accumulation):

• $\Delta k = s f(k) - (\delta + n)k$ (with population growth).
• Higher saving rate ($s$) → higher steady-state $k^*$ and $y^*$.
• Diminishing returns mean that the effect of saving decreases as $k$ rises.
• The golden rule saving rate ($s_{gold} = \alpha$ for Cobb-Douglas) maximizes consumption.

2. Population Growth:

• Higher $n$ → lower steady-state $k^*$ and $y^*$ (dilution).
• Break-even investment line is $(\delta + n)k$; higher $n$ rotates it up.
• Countries with high population growth tend to have lower income per capita.

3. Technological Progress:

• $Y = F(K, AL)$ where $A$ is technology (labor-augmenting).
• In per-effective-worker terms, $\tilde{k} = K/(AL)$, $\tilde{y} = f(\tilde{k})$.
• Steady-state growth rate of output per worker = $g$ (rate of technological progress).
• Technology is the ultimate source of sustained growth.

4. Human Capital:

• Augmented Solow model: $Y = K^\alpha H^\beta (AL)^{1-\alpha-\beta}$.
• Higher human capital ($H$) increases output and steady-state income.
• Education spending, health, and skills are investments in human capital.

5. Institutions:

• Property rights, rule of law, contract enforcement, honest government.
• Good institutions encourage investment and innovation.
• Poor institutions (corruption, expropriation) deter growth.

6. Trade Openness:

• Access to global markets increases competition, technology transfer, and scale.
• Open economies grow faster (empirical evidence).

7. Natural Resources:

• Resource-rich countries can grow faster (Canada, Australia, Norway).
• Resource curse: some resource-rich countries have poor institutions and slow growth (e.g., Venezuela, Nigeria).

Policy Implications:

• Promote saving and investment (tax incentives, infrastructure).
• Control population growth (family planning, education, women's empowerment).
• Invest in R&D and technology adoption (innovation policy, education).
• Build human capital (education, training, health).
• Strengthen institutions (rule of law, property rights, anti-corruption).
• Open to trade (free trade agreements, reduce barriers).
key_concept.context: The Solow model (1956) identified saving, population growth, and technology as the key factors. The augmented Solow model (Mankiw, Romer, Weil, 1992) added human capital. The role of institutions was emphasized by North (1990) and Acemoglu, Johnson, and Robinson (2001). Cross-country growth regressions have confirmed that these factors matter. For Canada, growth has been driven by capital accumulation (investment), labor force growth (immigration), and TFP (technology). Canada's TFP growth has been weak, explaining the productivity gap with the US.

image.alt: Diagram: Augmented Solow model with human capital; production function includes physical capital (K), human capital (H), and labor (L).
image.caption: Figure 13.4.3: The augmented Solow model includes human capital (education, skills) as an additional factor of production, explaining differences in growth across countries.

classifications.value: Higher saving leads to higher steady-state output per worker (level effect, not growth effect)
characteristics: Saving rate ($s$) determines how much output is invested.
Higher $s$ → higher $k^*$ and $y^*$ in steady state.
Effect is subject to diminishing returns (smaller impact at high $k$).
Growth increases temporarily during transition to new steady state.
Golden rule saving rate ($s_{gold} = \alpha$) maximizes consumption.
Empirical evidence: countries with high investment rates grow faster.
classifications.behavior:
Saving and investment are the primary drivers of capital accumulation. A higher saving rate means more resources are devoted to building new capital (machines, factories, infrastructure). This increases the capital stock per worker, raising output per worker. However, diminishing returns mean that each additional unit of capital produces less additional output. Eventually, the economy reaches a steady state where investment just covers depreciation (and population growth). At that point, growth stops (without technology).

The transition dynamics are important: when a country increases its saving rate, it experiences a period of faster growth as it accumulates capital toward the new steady state. This is why China's high saving rate (over 40% of GDP) contributed to its rapid growth. However, the growth effect is temporary; once the new steady state is reached, growth returns to the rate of technological progress.

For Canada, the saving rate is about 20-25% of GDP. Increasing the saving rate would raise steady-state output per worker, but the effect would be limited by diminishing returns. Estimates suggest that a 5 percentage point increase in the saving rate would increase steady-state output per worker by about 10-15%.

image.alt: Graph: Steady-state output per worker as function of saving rate; increasing but concave (diminishing returns).
image.caption: Figure 13.4.4: The relationship between the saving rate and steady-state output per worker. Higher saving leads to higher output, but with diminishing returns.

examples.explanation: China's saving rate has been over 40% of GDP for decades. This has fueled rapid capital accumulation and high growth (8-10% per year). As China's capital stock has grown, diminishing returns have set in, and growth has slowed to 4-5%.

Canada's saving rate is about 22% of GDP. This is moderate compared to other developed countries. Increasing the saving rate (e.g., through tax incentives for investment) could boost Canada's steady-state output per worker.

classifications.value: Higher population growth reduces steady-state output per worker (dilution effect)
characteristics: Population growth ($n$) dilutes capital per worker.
Break-even investment line: $(\delta + n)k$.
Higher $n$ → lower $k^*$ and $y^*$.
Countries with high population growth tend to be poorer.
Empirical evidence: negative correlation between population growth and income per capita.
However, population growth can also bring benefits (young workforce, innovation).
classifications.behavior:
Population growth affects capital per worker through dilution. When population grows, the existing capital stock must be spread across more workers, reducing capital per worker. In the Solow model, the break-even investment line is $(\delta + n)k$. A higher $n$ rotates this line upward, reducing the steady-state capital stock.

This explains why many developing countries with high population growth (e.g., Sub-Saharan Africa) have low income per capita. However, the relationship is not deterministic: some countries with moderate population growth (e.g., China, India) have grown rapidly by increasing saving and investment even more.

For Canada, population growth is about 1% per year, driven largely by immigration. Immigration brings benefits: younger workers, higher skills, and entrepreneurship. The dilution effect is offset by the contribution of immigrants to innovation and productivity. The Solow model's simple dilution effect does not capture these benefits, so the net effect of immigration on growth can be positive.

image.alt: Graph: Solow diagram showing effect of higher population growth (n1 to n2); break-even line rotates up; steady-state k* falls.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.5: Higher population growth reduces steady-state capital per worker (dilution effect). The break-even investment line rotates upward, lowering the steady-state capital stock.

examples.explanation: Many Sub-Saharan African countries have population growth rates of 2-3% per year. This dilutes capital per worker, contributing to low income per capita. Family planning and education (especially for girls) can reduce population growth and boost growth.

Canada's population growth is about 1% per year, one of the highest among developed countries. Immigration brings skilled workers, entrepreneurs, and innovation. The dilution effect is offset by these benefits, so Canada's growth has not been harmed by population growth.

classifications.value: Technological progress is the ultimate source of sustained long-run growth
characteristics: Technology ($A$) shifts the production function upward.
In the Solow model with technology, steady-state growth rate of output per worker = $g$ (rate of technological progress).
Technology includes innovation (new products, processes) and adoption (diffusion).
R&D spending, patents, and education are inputs to technological progress.
Empirical evidence: TFP growth (Solow residual) accounts for a large share of growth in developed countries.
Policies: R&D tax credits, innovation clusters, education, intellectual property protection.
classifications.behavior:
Technological progress is the ultimate source of sustained growth. Unlike capital accumulation, which faces diminishing returns, technological progress can continue indefinitely. In the Solow model with labor-augmenting technological progress ($Y = F(K, AL)$), the steady-state growth rate of output per worker equals the rate of technological progress ($g$).

Technological progress comes from two sources: innovation (creating new technologies) and adoption (diffusing existing technologies). Developing countries can grow rapidly by adopting technologies from advanced countries (catch-up growth). Advanced countries must rely on innovation to sustain growth.

For Canada, TFP growth has been weak (about 0.5% per year) compared to the US (about 1.0% per year). This explains the widening productivity gap. To boost TFP growth, Canada needs to increase R&D spending (currently 1.6% of GDP vs. 3.0% in the US), improve technology adoption, and strengthen innovation ecosystems.

image.alt: Graph: Output per worker over time with technological progress; upward trend with fluctuations.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.6: Technological progress shifts the production function upward, allowing sustained growth in output per worker. This is the ultimate source of long-run growth.

examples.explanation: The US has high R&D spending (3% of GDP), strong universities, and a vibrant innovation ecosystem. TFP growth has averaged about 1% per year, driving sustained growth in output per worker. The IT revolution (1995-2005) saw TFP growth surge to 1.5%.
Canada spends only 1.6% of GDP on R&D, well below the US (3.0%) and OECD average (2.5%). Canadian business R&D is particularly low. This contributes to slower TFP growth and the productivity gap.

classifications.value: Education, good institutions, and trade openness are important complementary factors
characteristics: Human capital: education, skills, health. Increases output and productivity.
Institutions: property rights, rule of law, honest government. Encourage investment and innovation.
Trade openness: access to global markets increases competition, technology transfer, and scale.
Empirical evidence: these factors are strongly correlated with growth.
Policies: education spending, legal reform, trade agreements.
classifications.behavior:
The basic Solow model focuses on physical capital, but human capital (education, skills, health) is equally important. The augmented Solow model includes human capital as a separate factor of production. Countries with higher levels of education (e.g., South Korea, Finland) have grown faster. Education increases worker productivity and the ability to adopt new technologies.

Institutions matter because they affect the incentives to invest and innovate. Countries with secure property rights, rule of law, and honest government attract investment and foster entrepreneurship. Countries with corruption, expropriation risk, and weak contract enforcement deter investment and growth. The difference between North and South Korea is a stark example: both had similar starting points, but South Korea developed good institutions and grew rapidly, while North Korea did not.

Trade openness exposes domestic firms to competition, forcing them to become more efficient. It also allows access to foreign technology and larger markets (economies of scale). Countries that have opened to trade (e.g., China, Vietnam) have grown rapidly. Canada's trade with the US (USMCA) has been a major driver of growth, though internal trade barriers remain a problem.

For Canada, policies to boost growth include: investing in education and skills, strengthening institutions (already strong), reducing internal trade barriers, and promoting innovation and R&D.

image.alt: Graph: Growth rate vs. years of schooling; positive correlation.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.7: Human capital (education) is strongly correlated with economic growth. Countries with higher levels of education tend to grow faster.

examples.explanation: South Korea invested heavily in education from the 1960s onward. Literacy rates rose from 70% to near 100%. The educated workforce enabled the adoption of advanced technologies and rapid growth. South Korea's income per capita rose from $1,500 in 1960 to over $30,000 today.

Canada's trade with the US (USMCA) has been a major driver of growth. Exports to the US account for 20% of Canadian GDP. Trade has exposed Canadian firms to competition, forcing efficiency improvements, and allowed access to a large market (economies of scale).

principles.principle: Technological Progress, Not Capital Accumulation, Is the Ultimate Source of Sustained Long-Run Growth
principles.statement: The Solow model shows that capital accumulation alone cannot sustain long-run growth because of diminishing returns. As the capital stock increases, the marginal product of capital falls. Eventually, investment just covers depreciation, and capital per worker stops growing. In contrast, technological progress shifts the production function upward, allowing output per worker to increase even with constant capital per worker. In the Solow model with technological progress, the steady-state growth rate of output per worker equals the rate of technological progress. This is why developed countries focus on innovation, R&D, and education—not just investment. Without technological progress, growth would eventually stop (as it did for most of human history before the Industrial Revolution).
principles.application: Policymakers should prioritize policies that promote technological progress: R&D tax credits, funding for basic research, support for innovation clusters, education and skills training, and intellectual property protection. While investment in physical capital is important, it cannot sustain growth indefinitely. Canada's weak TFP growth is a concern; policies to boost innovation and technology adoption are essential.
principles.example: Japan's rapid growth from 1950 to 1990 was driven by both capital accumulation (high saving) and technological catch-up (adopting US technology). As Japan approached the technological frontier, growth slowed to 0.5-1.0% per year, driven by its own innovation. This illustrates the shift from capital-driven to technology-driven growth.

image.alt: Graph: Output per worker with and without technological progress; without technology, growth stops; with technology, sustained growth.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.8: Without technological progress, growth eventually stops as the economy reaches the steady state. With technological progress, sustained growth is possible.

principles.principle: Conditional Convergence: Poor Countries Grow Faster Only If They Have Similar Steady States
principles.statement: The Solow model predicts conditional convergence: poor countries grow faster than rich countries only if they have similar saving rates, population growth rates, and technology (i.e., similar steady states). If a poor country has a low saving rate, high population growth, or poor institutions, its steady state is low, and it may not catch up. This explains why some poor countries (China, South Korea) have grown rapidly, while others (many African countries) have not. Convergence is conditional on policies and institutions. For Canada, convergence with the US is conditional on Canada having similar steady-state determinants. Canada's lower R&D spending and internal trade barriers may explain why the productivity gap persists.
principles.application: Policies that raise the steady state (higher saving, lower population growth, better institutions, technology adoption) can help poor countries converge. For Canada, to close the productivity gap with the US, Canada needs to raise its steady state through policies that boost productivity (R&D, internal trade, competition).
principles.example: South Korea and Ghana had similar GDP per capita in 1960 (around \$1,500). South Korea had high saving, low population growth, good institutions, and high education; Ghana had low saving, high population growth, and poor institutions. South Korea grew rapidly; Ghana grew slowly. This is conditional convergence.

image.alt: Scatter plot: Growth rate vs. initial GDP per capita, controlling for saving, population growth, education; negative relationship shows conditional convergence.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.9: Conditional convergence: when controlling for saving rates, population growth, and human capital, poorer countries tend to grow faster.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Effects of Saving and Population Growth on Steady State

image.alt: Table: Baseline (s=0.25, n=0.01), high saving (s=0.35), high population growth (n=0.03); calculations of k*, y*.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.10: Worked example: calculating the effects of changes in saving rate and population growth on steady-state capital and output.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has a Cobb-Douglas production function $y = k^{\alpha}$ with $\alpha = 0.33$. The depreciation rate is $\delta = 0.05$. Initially, the saving rate is $s = 0.25$ and population growth is $n = 0.01$.

a) Calculate the steady-state capital per worker ($k^*$) and output per worker ($y^*$).
b) If the saving rate increases to $s = 0.35$, calculate the new $k^*$ and $y^*$.
c) If population growth increases to $n = 0.03$ (with $s = 0.25$), calculate the new $k^*$ and $y^*$.
d) Compare the effects: which has a larger impact on steady-state output, a 10 percentage point increase in saving or a 2 percentage point increase in population growth?
e) Explain why the effects differ.

step_by_step.explanation:
A 10 percentage point increase in the saving rate (from 0.25 to 0.35) increases output per worker by 17.8%. A 2 percentage point increase in population growth (from 0.01 to 0.03) decreases output per worker by 13.4%. The saving effect is slightly larger in magnitude.

The effects differ because the saving rate affects the numerator of the steady-state formula, while population growth affects the denominator. The exponent $1/(1-\alpha) \approx 1.5$ amplifies both effects. The sensitivity depends on the initial levels.

The steady-state output per worker is $y^* = (s/(\delta+n))^{\alpha/(1-\alpha)}$. The elasticity of $y^*$ with respect to $s$ is $\alpha/(1-\alpha) \approx 0.5$, and with respect to $n$ is $-\alpha/(1-\alpha) \times (n/(\delta+n))$. The effect of $n$ is smaller when $\delta$ is large relative to $n$. In this example, $\delta=0.05$, $n$ increased from 0.01 to 0.03, so the effect is moderate. In countries with very high $n$ (e.g., 0.03-0.04), the effect of population growth can be larger.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) $k^* = 8.41$, $y^* = 2.02$. b) $k^* = 13.90$, $y^* = 2.38$ (17.8% increase). c) $k^* = 5.47$, $y^* = 1.75$ (13.4% decrease). d) Saving increase has slightly larger impact. e) Elasticities differ; $s$ affects numerator, $n$ affects denominator.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the Solow model to calculate the effects of changes in saving rate and population growth on steady-state capital and output per worker.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Contribution of TFP to Growth (Growth Accounting)

image.alt: Table: Output growth 2.5%, capital growth 2.0%, labor growth 1.0%, α=0.33; TFP calculation.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.11: Worked example: using growth accounting to decompose output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the following average annual growth rates over a decade:

• Output growth ($\%\Delta Y$): 2.5%
• Capital growth ($\%\Delta K$): 2.0%
• Labor growth ($\%\Delta L$): 1.0%
• Capital's share of income ($\alpha$): 0.33

a) Calculate the contribution of capital to output growth.
b) Calculate the contribution of labor to output growth.
c) Calculate the Solow residual (TFP growth).
d) What percentage of output growth is explained by TFP?
e) If the economy's TFP growth increased by 0.5 percentage points, how much would output growth increase?

step_by_step.explanation:
$\%\Delta Y = \%\Delta A + \alpha\%\Delta K + (1-\alpha)\%\Delta L$

$2.5\% = \%\Delta A + 0.66\% + 0.67\%$
$2.5\% = \%\Delta A + 1.33\%$
$\%\Delta A = 2.5\% - 1.33\% = 1.17\%$

TFP share = $1.17\% / 2.5\% = 46.8\%$

Almost half of output growth is due to technological progress (TFP), with the rest due to capital and labor accumulation.

step_by_step.step: Effect of 0.5 percentage point TFP increase
step_by_step.explanation:
If TFP growth increased by 0.5 percentage points (from 1.17% to 1.67%), output growth would increase by the same amount (assuming capital and labor growth unchanged).

New output growth = $2.5\% + 0.5\% = 3.0\%$

This illustrates the importance of TFP growth for overall growth. A small increase in TFP growth has a direct one-for-one effect on output growth.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 0.66%. b) 0.67%. c) 1.17%. d) 46.8%. e) Output growth would increase by 0.5 percentage points to 3.0%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying growth accounting to decompose output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP (technological progress).

process_flow.process: Identify the growth factor to analyze (saving, population, technology, human capital, institutions, trade)
process_flow.description: Determine which factor is of interest and how it is measured.
process_flow.mechanism: Saving rate (national accounts), population growth (demographics), TFP (growth accounting), education (years of schooling), institutions (governance indices), trade (exports+imports/GDP).

image.alt: Diagram: Growth factors (saving, population, technology, human capital, institutions, trade).
image.caption: Figure 13.4.12: Step 1 - Identify which growth factor to analyze (saving, population, technology, human capital, institutions, or trade).

process_flow.process: For saving/population: use Solow model to calculate steady-state effects
process_flow.description: Apply the Solow model formulas to determine how changes in $s$ or $n$ affect $k^*$ and $y^*$.

image.caption: Figure 13.4.13: Step 2 - For saving and population growth, use the Solow model to calculate steady-state effects.

process_flow.process: For technology: use growth accounting (Solow residual)
process_flow.description: Decompose output growth into contributions from capital, labor, and TFP.

image.caption: Figure 13.4.14: Step 3 - For technology, use growth accounting to estimate TFP growth (Solow residual).

process_flow.process: For human capital: use augmented Solow model
process_flow.description: Include human capital as an additional factor of production.

image.alt: Equation: Augmented Solow model with human capital.
image.caption: Figure 13.4.15: Step 4 - For human capital, use the augmented Solow model including education and skills.

process_flow.process: For institutions/trade: use cross-country regression evidence
process_flow.description: Use empirical evidence from cross-country growth regressions to assess the impact of institutions and trade.

image.caption: Figure 13.4.16: Step 5 - For institutions and trade, use cross-country regression evidence to estimate their impact on growth.

image.alt: Master framework: Identify factor → apply appropriate model (Solow for saving/population, growth accounting for technology, augmented Solow for human capital, regression for institutions/trade).
image.caption: Figure 13.4.17: Complete framework for analyzing factors affecting economic growth.

summary: Economic growth is determined by multiple factors. The Solow model identifies three primary drivers: saving (investment), population growth, and technological progress. Higher saving leads to higher steady-state output per worker (level effect) but does not affect the long-run growth rate. Higher population growth reduces steady-state output per worker (dilution effect). Technological progress is the ultimate source of sustained long-run growth; in the Solow model with technology, the steady-state growth rate of output per worker equals the rate of technological progress. The augmented Solow model adds human capital (education, skills), explaining differences in growth across countries. Institutions (property rights, rule of law, honest government) affect incentives to invest and innovate. Trade openness exposes firms to competition, facilitates technology transfer, and allows economies of scale. Natural resources can boost growth (Canada, Australia) but may also cause Dutch disease or resource curse if institutions are weak. For Canada, growth has been driven by moderate saving, immigration-driven population growth, and TFP (technology). Canada's TFP growth has lagged the US, explaining the productivity gap. Policies to boost growth include: promoting saving and investment, controlling population growth (where high), investing in R&D and technology adoption, building human capital, strengthening institutions, and opening to trade. Understanding these factors is essential for analyzing why some countries grow faster than others and for designing policies to promote long-run prosperity.
